children.name: Testudo
children.description: The Mediterranean tortoises — the classic domed, high-shelled land tortoises of southern Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East. Testudo species have been kept as pets for centuries and were traded extensively across the ancient world; Roman soldiers carried them as a food source on campaign. All are under significant conservation pressure from habitat loss, collection for the pet trade, and the slow reproductive rate typical of long-lived reptiles.

children.name: Testudo graeca
children.description: Testudo graeca (Spur-thighed Tortoise) is a mediterranean tortoises species found in Europe and Asia and Africa regions.

children.name: Testudo hermanni
children.description: Testudo hermanni (Hermann's Tortoise) is a mediterranean tortoises species found in Europe region.

children.name: Testudo marginata
children.description: Testudo marginata (Marginated Tortoise) is a mediterranean tortoises species found in Europe region.

children.name: Testudo kleinmanni
children.description: Testudo kleinmanni (Egyptian Tortoise) is a mediterranean tortoises species found in Africa and Asia regions.

children.name: Agrionemys
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Horsfield's Tortoise — the only tortoise of the Central Asian steppes, named after the naturalist Thomas Horsfield. Unlike its Mediterranean relatives it inhabits semi-arid grasslands and spends much of the year in torpor: active for only two to three months in spring, it spends the rest of the year in deep burrows escaping both summer heat and winter cold. It is the most widely kept tortoise in the world.

children.name: Agrionemys horsfieldii
children.description: Agrionemys horsfieldii (Horsfield's Tortoise) is a central asian tortoises species found in Asia region.

children.name: Kinixys
children.description: Hinged-back tortoises of sub-Saharan Africa — unique among tortoises in possessing a rear hinge on the carapace that allows the back of the shell to be lowered to protect the hindlimbs. Several species reach the savanna and open woodland at the southern edge of the Palearctic zone and are among the most ecologically diverse of African tortoises.

children.name: Kinixys erosa
children.description: Kinixys erosa (Serrated Hinged-back Tortoise) is a hinged-back tortoises species found in Africa region.

children.name: Kinixys belliana
children.description: Kinixys belliana (Bell's Hinged-back Tortoise) is a hinged-back tortoises species found in Africa region.

children.name: Kinixys homeana
children.description: A forest-dwelling hinged-back tortoise of West and Central Africa, named after the Scottish surgeon and naturalist Sir Everard Home. It inhabits lowland rainforest and wet forest edges, where it forages among leaf litter for fungi, fallen fruit, and invertebrates. Declining due to habitat loss and collection for the bushmeat and pet trades, it is listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN.

children.name: Kinixys lobatsiana
children.description: A small hinged-back tortoise restricted to rocky hillsides and open woodland in southeastern Botswana and adjacent South Africa, first described from specimens near Lobatse, Botswana. It forages on succulents, fallen fruits, and mushrooms and shelters under rocks or in termite mounds. Its very restricted range and ongoing habitat degradation place it in the Vulnerable category.

children.name: Kinixys natalensis
children.description: The smallest Kinixys species, confined to savanna thornbush and coastal forest in KwaZulu-Natal, Eswatini, and southern Mozambique. Its shell is noticeably flatter than related species, and the rear hinge develops later in life. Although locally common in suitable habitat, it faces pressure from the commercial pet trade.

children.name: Kinixys nogueyi
children.description: Distributed across West Africa from Senegal to Ghana and into the Congo Basin, this species inhabits moist savanna and forest margins. It is morphologically similar to K. belliana but distinguished by a more rounded carapace and geographic distribution. Heavy exploitation for bushmeat markets and local traditional medicine poses significant conservation concern.

children.name: Kinixys spekii
children.description: One of the most widespread Kinixys species, ranging from Tanzania and Kenya south to Zimbabwe and eastern South Africa in dry savanna and bushveld. Named after the explorer John Hanning Speke, it is a domed, medium-sized tortoise that forages on grasses, succulents, and carrion. It is considered Least Concern but is heavily collected in parts of its range.

children.name: Kinixys zombensis
children.description: Described from the Zomba Plateau of Malawi, this species ranges through coastal East Africa from southern Somalia to South Africa. It inhabits coastal forest, thicket, and savanna, and is somewhat more arboreal in behaviour than other Kinixys, occasionally climbing low shrubs. It is Under Revised Taxonomy reclassified from K. belliana subspecies and remains poorly studied.

children.name: Geochelone
children.description: Large continental tortoises with a broad distribution across South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa. The Indian Star Tortoise is named for the striking radiating yellow pattern on each scute — a pattern that breaks up the tortoise's outline in dappled grassland light and is among the most beautiful in the family.

children.name: Geochelone elegans
children.description: Geochelone elegans (Indian Star Tortoise) is a star tortoises species found in Asia region.

children.name: Geochelone platynota
children.description: Geochelone platynota (Burmese Star Tortoise) is a star tortoises species found in Asia region.

children.name: Aldabrachelys
children.description: A genus of giant tortoises from the western Indian Ocean, now represented by a single extant species on Aldabra Atoll in the Seychelles. Once distributed across Madagascar and other Indian Ocean islands, the surviving Aldabra giant tortoise is the world's largest land tortoise after the Galápagos species and one of the longest-lived animals on Earth.

children.name: Aldabrachelys gigantea
children.description: The world's largest surviving tortoise outside the Galápagos, reaching over 250 kg and living well beyond 150 years; the individual known as Adwaita may have exceeded 250 years. Native to Aldabra Atoll in the Seychelles, it grazes on grasses, herbs, and fallen fruit, playing a critical role as a megaherbivore and seed disperser on the atoll. Introduced breeding populations exist on Mauritius and the Seychelles mainland, partly restoring the ecological function of extinct giant tortoises.

children.name: Astrochelys
children.description: Two spectacularly patterned Malagasy giant tortoises whose carapace scutes bear bold yellow star-burst radiations on a dark background. Both species are endemic to Madagascar and face extreme pressure from habitat destruction and collection, making them among the most critically threatened tortoises on Earth.

children.name: Astrochelys radiata
children.description: Widely regarded as one of the world's most beautiful tortoises, the radiated tortoise bears a high-domed shell with bold yellow star-patterns on each scute, endemic to the dry spiny forests and scrublands of southern Madagascar. It can live over 100 years and may reach 40 cm in shell length. Although once abundant, catastrophic poaching and habitat loss have caused population crashes; the IUCN lists it as Critically Endangered.

children.name: Astrochelys yniphora
children.description: Possibly the world's rarest tortoise, restricted to a tiny area of dry bamboo scrub near Baly Bay in northwestern Madagascar, with wild population estimates sometimes below 500 adults. It is named for the distinctive forward-projecting gular scute on the plastron resembling a plowshare. Critically Endangered, it is subject to intensive conservation breeding and anti-poaching programs by the Durrell Wildlife Conservation Trust.

children.name: Centrochelys
children.description: A genus erected for the African spurred tortoise, the largest mainland tortoise on Earth and the third-largest tortoise species overall. Previously placed in Geochelone, it inhabits the arid Sahel belt of sub-Saharan Africa and is characterised by prominent spurred scales on the hindlimbs and a thick, yellowish-brown shell.

children.name: Centrochelys sulcata
children.description: The largest mainland tortoise in the world, routinely exceeding 80 kg and occasionally surpassing 100 kg, inhabiting the Sahel from Senegal to Ethiopia along the southern edge of the Sahara. It excavates deep burrows up to 3 m long to escape the extreme heat of the semi-desert and emerges to graze on grasses and succulents. Despite its Vulnerable IUCN status due to habitat loss and overgrazing, it is a popular pet species and populations are maintained in many zoos worldwide.

children.name: Chelonoidis
children.description: South American and Galápagos tortoises — a diverse genus whose ancestors floated across the Atlantic from Africa to South America in the Oligocene. The genus includes some of the most iconic megafauna of the Western Hemisphere: the enormous Galápagos tortoises and the red- and yellow-footed tortoises of the South American rainforest.

children.name: Chelonoidis carbonarius
children.description: A medium-sized tortoise of tropical South America and some Caribbean islands, immediately recognisable by the red or orange scales on its head and limbs that contrast with a dark, slightly oblong carapace. It inhabits seasonally dry tropical forests and savannas from Panama to Argentina, consuming a wide variety of fruits, leaves, fungi, and carrion. One of the most popular tortoise species in captivity globally, it remains broadly common in the wild though subject to heavy hunting pressure for meat.

children.name: Chelonoidis denticulatus
children.description: The largest tortoise in South America, reaching up to 82 cm and 60 kg, with yellow-orange head scales and a dark brown carapace; inhabits Amazonian and Orinoco basin rainforests from Colombia and Venezuela south to Bolivia and Brazil. It is a keystone seed disperser for large-seeded rainforest trees and plays an important ecological role similar to that of mega-herbivores elsewhere. Classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN, it is heavily hunted for food and the local pet trade across its range.

children.name: Chelonoidis chilensis
children.description: The southernmost tortoise in the Americas, inhabiting the dry Chaco and Monte scrublands of Argentina, Bolivia, and Paraguay, with isolated populations formerly attributed to Chile (where it is now extirpated). It has a low-domed, yellowish-brown carapace and tolerates wide temperature swings, entering semi-arid dormancy during the hottest and coldest months. Vulnerable per the IUCN, it faces severe pressure from habitat conversion to agriculture and illegal collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Chelonoidis abingdonii
children.description: Declared functionally extinct when its last known representative, the famous tortoise 'Lonesome George', died on 24 June 2012 at the Charles Darwin Research Station. Endemic to Pinta Island in the Galápagos, it had a distinctive saddleback carapace allowing it to reach high vegetation. Although genetically distinct individuals with partial Pinta ancestry have been found on Isabela Island, the pure lineage is considered extinct.

children.name: Chelonoidis becki
children.description: The largest of the Galápagos tortoise lineages, inhabiting the slopes of Volcán Wolf on northern Isabela Island; some individuals exceed 180 kg and 1.5 m in carapace length. Its domed carapace reflects the lush, humid highland vegetation available on the volcano's flanks. Conservation programs have successfully increased its numbers significantly from a low of around 1,000 individuals in the 1970s.

children.name: Chelonoidis chathamensis
children.description: Endemic to San Cristóbal (Chatham) Island in the Galápagos, the island where Charles Darwin first landed in 1835, this lineage was reduced to a few hundred animals by introduced predators and past over-hunting. It has a domed carapace and inhabits the island's central highlands. Active conservation and captive breeding at the Charles Darwin Research Station have substantially stabilised the population.

children.name: Chelonoidis darwini
children.description: Inhabiting Santiago (James) Island in the Galápagos, this lineage was nearly wiped out by nineteenth-century whalers and the introduced pigs and goats that devastated its habitat and consumed its eggs and hatchlings. Following large-scale feral animal eradication programs, the population is now considered to be recovering. It bears a moderately domed carapace and is named in honour of Charles Darwin.

children.name: Chelonoidis duncanensis
children.description: An isolated lineage found on tiny Pinzón (Duncan) Island in the central Galápagos, distinguished by its intermediate saddleback carapace shape. Introduced black rats preyed so heavily on eggs and hatchlings that no natural recruitment occurred for over 150 years; all wild adults dated to pre-rat-arrival cohorts. An intensive rat-eradication program completed in 2012 has since allowed successful hatching and juvenile survival.

children.name: Chelonoidis guntheri
children.description: Endemic to the Sierra Negra volcano on southern Isabela Island, one of the largest of the Galápagos volcanoes; this lineage has a distinctive flat-backed or slightly saddlebacked carapace suited to browsing in the island's drier zone. The lineage was named after the German-British herpetologist Albert Günther. Population estimates range around 600–700 individuals following recovery work.

children.name: Chelonoidis hoodensis
children.description: Endemic to Española (Hood) Island in the southeastern Galápagos and one of the most extreme saddleback lineages, with a highly flared, elongated anterior carapace allowing the tortoise to raise its head to reach cactus pads and shrubs. The population crashed to only 14 individuals in the 1960s; intensive captive breeding at the Darwin Station with a single male called Diego produced thousands of offspring that were successfully returned to Española.

children.name: Chelonoidis microphyes
children.description: Found on the slopes of Volcán Darwin on Isabela Island, this lineage takes its name from the ancient Greek for 'small-natured' despite being a large tortoise reaching over 100 kg; the name refers to its proportionally smaller size compared to the neighbouring Volcán Wolf lineage. It inhabits the arid western flanks of the volcano and remains relatively understudied compared to better-known Galápagos lineages.

children.name: Chelonoidis phantasticus
children.description: Known for over a century from a single museum specimen collected in 1906 and long feared extinct, a living female was dramatically rediscovered on Fernandina Island in February 2019, prompting immediate conservation intervention. Fernandina is one of the most volcanically active islands on Earth, suggesting any surviving population must be very small. The lineage has the most extreme saddleback carapace of any Galápagos tortoise.

children.name: Chelonoidis porteri
children.description: One of two distinct tortoise lineages on Santa Cruz Island, occupying the western and central portions of the island and characterised by a large, high-domed carapace. Santa Cruz holds the Darwin Research Station, and many of the most famous 'ambassador' Galápagos tortoises seen by tourists belong to this lineage. This is the most numerous wild Galápagos tortoise population, estimated in the thousands.

children.name: Chelonoidis vandenburghi
children.description: The most numerous of all Galápagos tortoise lineages, with population estimates exceeding 5,000 individuals on the broad caldera of Volcán Alcedo on Isabela Island; the caldera itself provides an unusually flat habitat that supports a very dense tortoise population. This lineage has a broadly domed carapace and congregates around crater lake and mudpool areas to thermoregulate. It is named after the Dutch-American herpetologist John Van Denburgh.

children.name: Chelonoidis vicina
children.description: Inhabiting the Cerro Azul volcano on the southwestern tip of Isabela Island, this lineage has an intermediate to mildly saddleback carapace and was severely reduced by the eruption of Cerro Azul in 1998, which directly killed many individuals. Rescue and translocation efforts by Galápagos National Park rangers during the eruption saved hundreds of animals. Population size is estimated at around 700–900 individuals.

children.name: Chersina
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the angulate tortoise of southern Africa, one of the most common tortoises on the Cape Peninsula and the most abundant tortoise species in the Western Cape of South Africa. Its name derives from the Greek for 'of dry land', reflecting its preference for dry fynbos and Succulent Karoo habitats.

children.name: Chersina angulata
children.description: The most abundant tortoise of South Africa's Cape Floristic Region, instantly recognisable by its elongated, slightly triangular plastron that ends in a pointed gular projection used by males as a battering ram in combat. Males are notably aggressive during the breeding season, and bouts of combat in which rivals are overturned are commonly observed. It inhabits fynbos, Succulent Karoo, and coastal thicket from South Africa's southwest coast northward into Namibia, and is listed as Least Concern.

children.name: Chersobius
children.description: Tiny padloper tortoises of southern Africa — the smallest tortoises in the world. All three species were formerly placed in Homopus and are restricted to the arid and semi-arid regions of South Africa and Namibia. They are specialists of rocky outcrops and dwarf shrubland, and their flattened shells allow them to squeeze into narrow rock crevices.

children.name: Chersobius boulengeri
children.description: A tiny tortoise endemic to the rocky Karoo region of South Africa's Northern and Western Cape provinces, with a shell rarely exceeding 14 cm; its flattened carapace is reddish-brown with dark-bordered scutes. It specialises on hard, succulent dwarf shrubs of the Karoo biome and retreats into rock crevices during heat and drought. Named in honour of the Belgian-British herpetologist George Albert Boulenger, it is classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Chersobius signatus
children.description: The world's smallest tortoise species, with females reaching only 9.6 cm and males smaller still, inhabiting a tiny strip of Namaqualand succulent scrub on the west coast of South Africa and southern Namibia. Its orange-brown carapace is dotted with black spots on each scute, providing remarkable camouflage against lichen-covered quartzite rocks. It is listed as Near Threatened, primarily due to habitat destruction and illegal collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Chersobius solus
children.description: A small padloper endemic to the extremely arid succulent scrubland of southern Namibia (formerly known as Berger's Cape tortoise), adapted to very low rainfall and extreme temperatures. Its rounded carapace is uniformly tan to brown without the pronounced spotting of C. signatus. Classified as Vulnerable, it is threatened by overgrazing of its specialised habitat and by trade.

children.name: Gopherus
children.description: The gopher tortoises of North America — powerful burrowing tortoises of the arid and semi-arid regions of the United States and Mexico, named for their extensive burrow systems that provide critical shelter for hundreds of other species. As ecosystem engineers and keystone species, gopher tortoises are among the most ecologically important reptiles on the continent.

children.name: Gopherus agassizii
children.description: Inhabiting the Mojave and Sonoran Deserts of California, Nevada, Utah, and Arizona, this species is built for extreme aridity: it can store water in its oversized urinary bladder and withstands body temperatures that would kill most reptiles. It spends up to 95% of its life underground in burrows and is listed as Vulnerable due to habitat loss to urban sprawl, road mortality, and respiratory disease exacerbated by translocation stress. Named after the Swiss-American naturalist Louis Agassiz.

children.name: Gopherus berlandieri
children.description: The smallest Gopherus species, found in the dry thorn-scrub of southern Texas and northeastern Mexico (Tamaulipas and Nuevo León), where it favours sandy soils for digging short shallow burrows called pallets. Unlike other gopher tortoises it is a more selective feeder, specialising in the pads of prickly-pear cactus. Named for the French-Belgian naturalist Jean-Louis Berlandier, it is listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Gopherus evgoodei
children.description: Described as a distinct species in 2014, this tortoise inhabits the thornscrub and tropical dry forests of the Pacific slope of Sinaloa and northern Nayarit, Mexico — a habitat known as the Sinaloan thornscrub. It was previously confused with G. morafkai and is distinguished by genetic, morphological, and ecological differences. Named after the American herpetologist Ernest Edward Good, it is evaluated as Vulnerable.

children.name: Gopherus flavomarginatus
children.description: The largest North American tortoise, reaching up to 47 cm and 7 kg, endemic to the Chihuahuan Desert grasslands of the Bolsón de Mapimí region of Chihuahua, Coahuila, and Durango in northern Mexico. It digs some of the most elaborate burrow systems of any tortoise, up to 9 m long, which it shares with other wildlife. Critically Endangered due to habitat degradation and past hunting, reintroduction programs into the southwestern US are restoring its historical range.

children.name: Gopherus morafkai
children.description: Inhabiting the Sonoran Desert of Arizona and Sonora, Mexico, this species was split from G. agassizii in 2011 based on genetic data and differences in habitat preference; it favours rocky desert slopes and canyons rather than flat desert flats. Active during summer monsoon rains, it enters dormancy during the hottest and coldest months. Named for herpetologist David J. Morafka, it is listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Gopherus polyphemus
children.description: A keystone species of the longleaf pine ecosystem of the southeastern United States, whose burrows — up to 4.5 m deep and 9 m long — provide essential refuge for over 350 other species including indigo snakes, gopher frogs, and burrowing owls. The tortoise itself grazes on low-growing grasses and fruiting plants in fire-maintained open-canopy habitats. It is listed as Vulnerable in the US, largely due to longleaf pine habitat loss across more than 97% of its historical range.

children.name: Homopus
children.description: Small padloper tortoises of southern Africa, formerly encompassing the Chersobius species as well. The remaining Homopus species inhabit rocky succulent scrub in South Africa and southern Namibia, and are among the least studied tortoise genera due to their small size, secretive habits, and restricted distributions.

children.name: Homopus areolatus
children.description: The most widespread of the padloper tortoises, ranging through the Western and Eastern Cape provinces of South Africa in fynbos, thicket, and succulent Karoo habitats on both rocky and sandy soils. Males have a reddish-orange head and orange-red limbs and are considerably smaller than females, with flattened shells suited to squeezing under rock slabs. It is listed as Least Concern but faces ongoing habitat loss from agriculture and invasive alien plants.

children.name: Homopus femoralis
children.description: The largest padloper species, reaching up to 15 cm, inhabiting rocky dolerite and sandstone outcrops in the Great Karoo and southern Highveld of South Africa and Lesotho. Distinguished from H. areolatus by its larger size, more rounded carapace, and the presence of prominent tubercular scales on the upper thighs (hence femoralis). It is classified as Vulnerable due to its restricted range, low population densities, and susceptibility to collection.

children.name: Indotestudo
children.description: Forest tortoises of South and Southeast Asia — medium-sized tortoises of humid tropical and subtropical forests from India to the Indonesian archipelago. They tend to have flatter, more oval shells than the high-domed African and Mediterranean tortoises, and all three species are under severe pressure from hunting and the Asian food and medicine trade.

children.name: Indotestudo elongata
children.description: A large forest tortoise with a distinctively oblong carapace ranging from India and Nepal through Myanmar, Thailand, Vietnam, and the Malay Peninsula into southern China. During breeding season males develop a striking pink coloration on the nasal region and around the eyes. Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List, it is heavily collected for local consumption and the international wildlife trade, with its range fragmented and declining.

children.name: Indotestudo forstenii
children.description: Endemic to the islands of Sulawesi and Halmahera in the Indonesian archipelago, this species was long confused with I. elongata but was validated as a distinct species by morphological and genetic analysis. It inhabits moist tropical forest and is among the least-studied of the Asian tortoises. Named for the Dutch naturalist Eltjo Alegondas Forsten, it is listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Indotestudo travancorica
children.description: Restricted to the wet evergreen forests of the Western Ghats of Kerala and Tamil Nadu in southwest India, named after the historical princely state of Travancore. It is a medium-sized, domed forest tortoise that browses on fallen fruits, mushrooms, and plant material in the forest understorey. Classified as Vulnerable, it faces threats from deforestation and collection; it is considered sacred in some local traditions.

children.name: Malacochersus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the pancake tortoise — unique among tortoises for its remarkably flat, highly flexible shell. The bones of the carapace are incomplete and paper-thin, allowing the animal to inflate its lungs to wedge itself into rock crevices rather than withdrawing into its shell when threatened.

children.name: Malacochersus tornieri
children.description: Inhabiting rocky kopjes (rock outcrops) in the dry thornbush of Tanzania and Kenya, the pancake tortoise can reach a crevice with remarkable speed — the fastest-moving tortoise in the world for short distances. Its flat, lightweight shell is so flexible that a living animal can be significantly compressed by hand, but the pliable structure is no defensive advantage against mammalian predators. Named after the German herpetologist Gustav Tornier, it is Vulnerable due to heavy collection for the international pet trade.

children.name: Manouria
children.description: The Asian giant tortoises — the two largest tortoises of mainland Asia, inhabiting the upland forests of South and Southeast Asia. Manouria is thought to be the most primitive living genus of tortoises based on molecular phylogenetics, retaining several ancestral skeletal features. Both species are Endangered and declining rapidly due to forest loss and consumption.

children.name: Manouria emys
children.description: The largest tortoise in mainland Asia, reaching 60 cm and 37 kg, inhabiting humid tropical montane forests from northeast India, Bangladesh, and Myanmar through Thailand, Malaysia, and Indonesia. Unusually among tortoises, females construct a nest mound of vegetation and actively guard the eggs — a behaviour more reminiscent of some lizards and crocodilians. Endangered on the IUCN Red List, it is consumed heavily across its range and faces serious habitat loss.

children.name: Manouria impressa
children.description: A medium-sized tortoise with a distinctive flattened, 'impressed' carapace that is concave between the vertebral scutes, giving it an unusual ridged appearance; it inhabits cool, moist montane forest at elevations above 900 m in Myanmar, Thailand, Vietnam, Laos, and southern China. Less well-studied than M. emys, it is also Vulnerable and similarly threatened by hunting and deforestation across its fragmented range.

children.name: Psammobates
children.description: Three small, patterned tortoises of southern Africa, endemic to the Fynbos and Succulent Karoo biomes of South Africa; collectively known as tent or geometric tortoises. Each scute on their carapaces bears distinct radiating pale lines on a dark background, providing extraordinary camouflage among dry vegetation. All three species have limited distributions and face conservation challenges.

children.name: Psammobates geometricus
children.description: One of the world's most endangered tortoises, restricted to a tiny fragment of renosterveld (a critically threatened shrubland) in the Western Cape of South Africa, with perhaps fewer than 3,000 individuals remaining in heavily fragmented populations. Its hexagonal, star-patterned carapace is exceptionally beautiful and made it a target of collection. It is classified as Critically Endangered and is a flagship species for renosterveld conservation.

children.name: Psammobates oculifer
children.description: Inhabiting the arid Kalahari sandveld and Nama Karoo of South Africa, Botswana, and southern Namibia, this small tortoise has a strongly serrated carapace edge and bold caramel-and-black patterning. Unlike the other Psammobates it tolerates a wider range of habitats and is more broadly distributed, though it remains poorly studied. It is currently evaluated as Least Concern.

children.name: Psammobates tentorius
children.description: The most widespread Psammobates species, distributed across the Succulent Karoo and Nama Karoo of South Africa and southern Namibia, with a distinctly tent-shaped or wedge-like carapace profile when viewed from the side. Three subspecies are recognised, differing in shell shape and patterning across their geographic ranges. It is listed as Near Threatened, threatened by overgrazing and illegal collection.

children.name: Pyxis
children.description: Two small, highly distinctive Malagasy tortoises — the spider tortoise and the flat-tailed spider tortoise — endemic to the arid southwestern coast of Madagascar. Both species have a remarkable hinge on the plastron (unusual in non-Kinixys tortoises) that allows females to close the front lobe over the limbs. Both are Critically Endangered.

children.name: Pyxis arachnoides
children.description: Named for the intricate web-like yellow lines radiating across its dark carapace (resembling a spider's web), this small tortoise is found in the spiny thicket of southwestern Madagascar. It is remarkable for entering a prolonged dry-season dormancy buried in sandy soil, active for only a few months of the year. Critically Endangered due to habitat loss, drought, and illegal collection.

children.name: Pyxis planicauda
children.description: Endemic to a tiny strip of coastal dry deciduous forest in the Menabe region of western Madagascar, this species is distinguished from P. arachnoides by its noticeably flattened tail and the absence of a plastral hinge. One of the rarest tortoises in the world, it is estimated that only a few thousand individuals remain in a habitat that has been severely degraded by logging and charcoal production. It is listed as Critically Endangered.

children.name: Stigmochelys
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the leopard tortoise — the largest tortoise in Africa after the sulcata and one of the most widespread, occurring across sub-Saharan savannas from Sudan and Ethiopia south to the Cape. The leopard tortoise was placed in Geochelone for many years but molecular data confirms it forms its own distinct lineage.

children.name: Stigmochelys pardalis
children.description: Africa's second-largest mainland tortoise, reaching up to 70 cm and 54 kg, with a boldly patterned carapace of black spots and radiations on yellow or tan ground — the 'leopard' pattern giving it both camouflage and its common name. It ranges across open savanna and bushveld from Ethiopia and Sudan south to the Cape, grazing on a wide variety of grasses, succulents, and thistles. Highly popular in the international pet trade, it is listed as Least Concern but faces localised pressure from collection and road mortality.
